title: Molecular epidemiology of potential candidate markers for chloroquine resistance in imported Plasmodium vivax malaria cases in Iran.
author_string: Pirahmadi, Sakineh, Afzali, Shima, Mehrizi, Akram Abouie, Raz, Abbasali, Raeisi, Ahmad
abstract: The spread of Plasmodium vivax strains resistant to chloroquine (CQ) has posed a challenge to control strategies aimed at eliminating malaria. Molecular analysis of candidate resistance markers is very important for monitoring the P. vivax resistance to CQ in different endemic regions. In the present study, the multidrug resistance 1 (pvmdr1) gene, a possible marker for CQ resistance in P. vivax, was evaluated by molecular methods. A simple PCR-RFLP method was developed for mutation analysis in pvmdr1 gene. A number of 120 blood spots were obtained from patients with P. vivax mono-infection in 2021. All of the samples were collected from Pakistani patients who travelled to Iran. None of the samples had any mutation at codon 976 of pvmdr1, while the 1076 mutation was detected in 96.2% of the examined isolates. Only two pvmdr1 haplotypes were identified, including the single mutant (Y976/1076L) as the most prevalent haplotype (with 96.2% frequency) and the wild type (Y976/F1076; with 3.8% frequency). In this study, the major CQ resistance-mediating mutation and multiple mutant haplotypes of the pvmdr1 gene was not detected. However, continuous monitoring of drug resistance markers and close supervision of the efficacy of CQ is essential to detect the potential emergence of CQ-resistant P. vivax isolates in Iran. This data is important for performing future epidemiological surveillance to monitor CQ resistance in this endemic area and the bordering regions.
